coredata.dc:title: Boron nitride quantum dots-enhanced laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry analysis and imaging of bisphenol A
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yanfang
Boukherroub, Rabah
Liu, Lu
Li, Huizhi
Zhao, Ru-Song
Wei, Qin
Yu, Xiang
Chen, Xiangfeng
coredata.dc:description: Bisphenol A (BPA) displays harmful effects on the human health, including potent endocrine activity and potential impact on the development of cancer. Analysis BPA residues in water and plastic products attracted considerable attention in the past decades. However, dominantly used conventional analysis techniques are unable to directly and non-destructively identify the correct species of BPA in plastic products. Hence, this study demonstrates the effective utilisation of boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) as an inorganic matrix in matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry analysis and imaging (MALDI-MS & MSI) for BPA. The presence of abundant hydroxyl and amino groups on the BNQDs’ surface is favourable for the formation of hydrogen bonds with BPA, and increases their ionization and chemoselectivity. Intriguingly, the BNQDs matrix offers a distinct signal for phenolic hazardous molecules featuring different hydroxyl groups. The method was applied to detect BPA at nanomolar level in environmental water, and also allowed non-destructive and in situ mapping of BPA in plastics and pacifiers. This research provides a novel strategy for adapting nanomaterials as inorganic matrices for analysis of small molecular pollutants in environmentally relevant samples using MALDI-MS & MSI.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bisphenol A
Boron nitride quantum dots
MALDI-MS
Imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spatial distribution of BPA analyzed by BNQDs-assisted MALDI-MSI

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074071, 51571183), and the Program for Taishan Scholars of Shandong Province (NO. tsqn 202103099, ts20190948), Small and medium-sized enterprises innovation ability improvement project (2023TSGC0055), Jinan University and Institute Innovation Team Project (2021GXRC090). The Pilot Project on the Integration of Science Education and Production (2022PY024 and 2022PYI013). The authors would like to thank profeesor Zhang from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the language editing.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074071, 51571183 ), and the Program for Taishan Scholars of Shandong Province (NO. tsqn 202103099, ts20190948 ), Small and medium-sized enterprises innovation ability improvement project ( 2023TSGC0055 ), Jinan University and Institute Innovation Team Project ( 2021GXRC090 ). The Pilot Project on the Integration of Science Education and Production ( 2022PY024 and 2022PYI013 ). The authors would like to thank profeesor Zhang from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the language editing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images, (c) Size distribution, (d) Ultraviolet-visible spectrum, (e) FTIR spectrum, and (f) XPS survey and core level spectra of (g) N 1 s, and (h) B 1 s of BNQDs.
MALDI mass spectra of 2,2-DP, 2,2-PP and BPA assisted by (a) BNQDs and (c) DHB matrix, AQ, 1-hydroxyl-AQ and 1,5-dihydroxyl-AQ assisted by (b) BNQDs and (d) DHB matrix. BPA concentration: 2.0 µM.

(a) Calibration curves used for the quantitative determination of BPA. Mass spectra of BPA obtained from MALDI measurements using (b) BNQDs as a matrix, and (c) liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry.

MALDI-MS images of the spatial BPA distribution (m/z 227.14) in thermally treated (0–72 h at 130 °C) epoxy resin films (a, b, c); The absolute intensity change of BPA with release time (d); MALDI-MS images of the spatial distribution of BPA (e, f, g).
Desorption/ionization mechanism of BPA using BNQDs as a matrix.
Synthesis of BNQDs and their application as an inorganic matrix for BPA analysis by MALDI-MS.
BPA determination in water samples using BNQDs as a matrix.

head.ce:title: Boron nitride quantum dots-enhanced laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry analysis and imaging of bisphenol A

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bisphenol A (BPA) displays harmful effects on the human health, including potent endocrine activity and potential impact on the development of cancer. Analysis BPA residues in water and plastic products attracted considerable attention in the past decades. However, dominantly used conventional analysis techniques are unable to directly and non-destructively identify the correct species of BPA in plastic products. Hence, this study demonstrates the effective utilisation of boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) as an inorganic matrix in matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry analysis and imaging (MALDI-MS & MSI) for BPA. The presence of abundant hydroxyl and amino groups on the BNQDs’ surface is favourable for the formation of hydrogen bonds with BPA, and increases their ionization and chemoselectivity. Intriguingly, the BNQDs matrix offers a distinct signal for phenolic hazardous molecules featuring different hydroxyl groups. The method was applied to detect BPA at nanomolar level in environmental water, and also allowed non-destructive and in situ mapping of BPA in plastics and pacifiers. This research provides a novel strategy for adapting nanomaterials as inorganic matrices for analysis of small molecular pollutants in environmentally relevant samples using MALDI-MS & MSI.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is a common additive utilized in the production of epoxy resins and polycarbonate plastics [1,2]. Plastics are widely used in baby bottles, food can linings, water and beverage containers, and as well as other products [3,4]. Due to widespread use, BPA is found in food, water, and the environment [5,6]. However, BPA is known for its endocrine-disrupting properties, negatively impacting human growth, reproduction, and development [7,8]. It can also cause diseases of the endocrine system and increase the risk of cancer [9,10]. The United States, European Union, and China have increased their efforts to monitor and control BPA risk, because of the harm it can cause to human health. Therefore, it is important to develop highly accurate and sensitive methods for detecting and monitoring BPA. Conventional techniques for analyzing BPA include gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [11], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [12], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13]. These methods are accurate and sensitive, but they have certain limitations, such as high solvent consumption, complex pretreatment, and extended analysis time [14]. Therefore, the development of rapid and effective BPA detection methods is crucial.

A soft ionization mass spectrometry technology, matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization mass spectrometry (MALDI-MS), ionizes the target by transferring laser energy through the matrix to analytes being measured [15,16]. This method exhibits several advantages, including simple sample preparation, reduced consumption of organic solvents, rapid analysis, and high sensitivity [17,18]. These advantages make it an effective technique for analyzing small molecules [19,20]. Furthermore, MALDI-MS imaging (MALDI-MSI), a new molecular imaging technology, has many beneficial features, such as no label requirement, no complex sample pretreatment, and high throughput, which enables the direct analysis of the target and acquisition of spatial distribution information of the substance in tissue sections [21,22]. MALDI-MS & MSI techniques have become increasingly popular in recent years for analyzing small molecules in environmental media [23,24]. However, traditional organic matrices, such as α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid (CHCA) and 2, 5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2, 5-dihydroxybenzoicacid, 2, 5-DHB) are often decomposed upon laser irradiation, resulting in an enormous number of ions overlapping with the detection signals of analytes in the low mass range (< 800 Da), limiting the application of MALDI-MS & MSI in the analysis of low molecular weight compounds [25–27].

In recent decades, numerous efforts have been made to develop matrices using various nanomaterials to achieve low background noise and high MS intensity for analyzing small molecules rather than traditional organic matrices [28–30]. For instance, MALDI-MS analysis using quantum dots matrices displays higher salt tolerance and lower background interferences in the low-mass range [31]. In addition, it may provide a great number of electron-rich sites, facilitating analyte deprotonation [32]. In the recent years, a new class of zero-dimensional (0D) materials, called boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs), was reported [33]. BNQDs exhibit high thermal conductivity for applications in optoelectronics [34,35], have stable fluorescence sensing properties for sensing various analytes [36,37], own good conductivity for electrochemical sensor applications [38,39] and also display low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility for in vivo and in vitro biological imaging [40]. In addition, BNQDs feature a high UV–visible light absorption capacity, which is suitable to serve as MALDI-MS & MSI. Functionalization or hybridization of the BN-based materials can further improve their performance [41,42]. For example, some hybrid materials of boron nitride nanosheets further improve their own specific sensing properties, which make them better selectivity to achieve applications [43,44]. The long-term goal of our work is to enhance the performance of MALDI-MS and MALDI-MSI for analysis of small molecules. To achieve this goal, we carry out research work from two directions. 1) Development of nanomaterial based solid matrix; 2) Development of matrix deposition devices. In our previous work, we have synthesized fluorinated boron nitride nanosheet (F-BNNs) from pristine BNNs [45]. The F-BNNSs were employed to enrich perfluoroalkyl acids via the specific fluorine–fluorine interactions, and then as an inorganic matrix for the MALDI-MS analysis. The F-BNNs increase both enrichment ability and ionization efficiency. A major limitation of nanosheets is that if they are not deposited on the substrate, they often form curly structures at the edges. This behavior might cause the adsorbed analytes to be partially wrapped and thus affect their ionization and transfer in the laser irradiation process. This work explored the application of BNQDs as solid-matrix for MALDI-MS and MSI analysis of BPA. Specifically, the presence of abundant amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of BNQDs endows them with a potential to selectively target BPA molecules containing hydroxyl groups.

In this study, we investigated the potential of BNQDs as an inorganic matrix for MALDI-MS analysis and imaging of BPA residues in different environmentally-relevant samples. The proposed MALDI-MS method is capable of discriminating between phenols with varying numbers of hydroxyl groups along with their structural isomers. The developed method is also applied to rapidly evaluate BPA in environmental water. Interestingly, the BNQDs matrix is able of direct and quantitative detection of BPA, both as a raw material residue and as an epoxy degradation product. In addition, the spatial distribution of BPA in pacifiers could be determined using MALDI-MSI. The mechanism of ionization of the hydroxy and amino groups present on the surface of BNQDs is discussed. To analyze small molecular pollutants in environmental samples, MALDI MS provides new opportunities for tailoring nanomaterial matrices for MALDI-MS & MSI.

Bisphenol A (BPA), anthraquinone (AQ), 1-hydroxyanthraquinone (1-hydroxyl-AQ,), 1,5-dihydroxyanthraquinone (1,5-dihydroxyl-AQ), 2,2-diphenylpropane (2,2-DP) and 2-phenyl-2-phenolpropane (2,2-PP) were purchased from Appel Experimental Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 5-Dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB), 9-aminoacridine (9-AA), and 1,5-naphthalenediamine (DAN) were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (Tokyo, Japan). N-naphthylethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NEDC) and norharmane were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). The liquid epoxy (EP) was provided by Shandong Deyuan Epoxy Technology Co. LTD. α-Cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid (CHCA) was bought from Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Boric acid (H3BO3) and ammonia (NH3‧H2O) were acquired from Chemreagent Chemical Reagent Group Co., Ltd. Methanol (MeOH) and acetonitrile (ACN) of chromatography grade were supplied from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water used throughout the experiments was obtained from a Millipore water dispenser (Billerica, MA, USA).

BNQDs rich in amino and hydroxyl groups were synthesized following the procedure in [46] with minor modifications. Briefly, 0.4 g of H3BO3 powder was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. Next, concentrated ammonia (0.6 mL) was added into the above solution. The mixed solution was degassed with nitrogen for 30 min and then transferred in a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 12 h. The autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature to provide NH2- and OH-rich BNQDs. The characterization techniques used to assess the morphology, microstructures and chemical composition of the synthesized BNQDs are described in details in the Supplementary Material section.

A concentration of 1 mmol L−1(mM) of each standard stock solution (BPA, AQ, 1-hydroxyl-AQ, 1,5-dihydroxyl-AQ, 2,2-DP and 2,2-PP) was prepared in ACN. Various diluted working solutions were prepared for further testing. Conventional organic matrices, HCCA and DHB (5.0 mg mL−1 each), were dissolved in a mixture of 30%ACN and 70% water (v/v). NEDC, DAN, Norharmane, and 9AA (5 mg mL−1 each) were dissolved in a mixture of 50%ACN and 50% water (v/v). All the obtained samples were stored at 4 °C before use.

In this research, the "matrix-mix" technique was used. In short, the matrix (10 μL) and analyte (10 μL) were properly mixed before 1 μL of the resulting mixture was applied to the target plate and allowed to dry.

Bottled water was obtained from a local market. The local sewage treatment system provided wastewater samples, and the detailed information of the local sewage treatment system was described in the Supplementary Material. We collected seawater samples from Bohai Sea, placed them in brown glass bottles and transported them back to the laboratory.

The water sample was passed through a 0.45 µm filter membrane. MALDI-MS analysis: The water (10 μL) and BNQDs matrix (10 μL) were mixed in advance, and then 1 μL of the mixture was pipetted onto the stainless-steel ground sample plate. Finally, the dried ground target plate was used for MALDI-MS detection. HPLC-MS/MS detection: 50 mL of water sample were enriched through a solid phase extraction column, then eluted with 5 and 3 mL of methanol successively; the eluent was dried by flowing nitrogen, finally redissolved into 1 mL, and 5 μL solutions was used for HPLC-MS/MS detection.

To prepare epoxy films, a mixture of ethylenediamine (0.5 g) and liquid epoxy (4.5 g) was sonicated for 30 min. A thin layer of the mixture was coated onto indium tin oxide (ITO) glass using bristle brush, and then cured at room temperature for 24 h. The obtained epoxy films were then subjected to thermal treatment in a vacuum drying oven at 130 °C for 0, 24, 48, and 72 h. Ethanol was used to clean the epoxy films. An High-LINE HP-CH model airbrush (Tokyo, Japan) was employed to spray the matrix. To obtain a thin layer of BNQDs in advance, five cycles of spraying (5 s of spraying followed by a drying time of 20 s) were applied. The distance between the epoxy film surface and the nozzle was maintained at 15 cm during spraying. The BNQD matrix solution was sprayed evenly for 15 cycles after air-drying for 10 min at room temperature in a fume hood.

Pacifiers made of silicone rubber were bought in a local store. They were cleaned with purified water and dried in air. Blank pacifiers and pacifiers used by babies after one year were stored at − 80 °C for three days before experiment. A Thermo Cryostar NX50 cryostat (Walldorf, Germany) was employed to perform slicing tasks. Sections (30 µm) of the used pacifiers were prepared and followed by mounting on an ITO slide. The matrix spray process was similar to that used for the epoxy films. Detailed sample preparation methods are described in the Supplementary Material.
The synthesis of BNQDs and their application as a matrix in the MALDI-MS analysis of BPA are depicted in Scheme 1.

To carry out the MALDI-MS and MSI tests, a RapifleX MALDI Tissuetyper™ TOF/TOF MS instrument (Bruker Daltonics, Billerica, MA, USA) with 355 nm radiation in the negative mode was used. At a laser frequency of 500 Hz, the MS data were acquired by capturing 500 laser shots. We collected raw mass spectra in 150–300 m/z range. The sample spatial resolution for MSI was set to 100 µm. Other MALDI-MS and MSI parameters were listed in the Supplementary Material. BPA was detected by high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) using a Thermo U-3000 (Dionex, CA, USA) HPLC system equipped with an Applied Biosystems Sciex QTrap 5500 (AB SCIEX, USA) MS/MS. The detailed experimental conditions for HPLC-MS/MS were listed in Supplementary Material.

The obtained MALDI-MS data were processed and analyzed using the Flex Analysis software (version 4.0, Bremen, Germany). The SCiLS 2018b software (Bremen, Germany) was used to analyze the imaging data. Processing of MSI results was performed by normalizing the total ion count (TIC).

Figs. 1a and 1b shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the as-prepared BNQDs which are homogeneously distributed and possess uniform lateral sizes. A lattice parameter of 0.22 nm corresponding to the in-plane lattice spacing of the (100) facet of hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets (h-BN) is visible in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the BNQDs ( Fig. 2a, inset), indicating good crystallinity. Particle size analysis (Fig. 1c) revealed BNQDs of 8 nm mean diameter. The BNQDs fabricated here are homogeneously distributed and have uniform lateral sizes, which are believed to be more favorable for imaging than the nanosheets. Fig. 1d depicts the UV–vis spectrum of BNQDs, featuring a strong and broad (300–550 nm) optical absorption, which makes BNQDs a suitable matrix in MALDI-MS to absorb and transfer laser energy to analytes. Excitation of the BNQDs in water at 365 nm resulted in blue luminescence (inset), demonstrating the successful production of BNQDs. The chemical composition of the BNQDs was further investigated using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. 1e). Characteristic peaks appeared, attributed to the O−H and N − H stretching vibrations at 3255 cm−1, which indicates successful passivation with functional moieties including −NH2 and −OH bonded to the edges or defect sites in the atomic structure of h-BN [39]. The hydroxyl and amino groups are abundant in BNQDs, allowing them to form hydrogen bonds with BPA and increase their ionization and chemo-selectivity. Four peaks were observed at 532.5, 401.5, 284.6, and 191.3 eV in the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) plot of the BNQDs (Fig. 1f), which assigned to O, N, C, and Brespectively. Fig. 1g exhibits the N 1 s fine spectrum, which could be fitted with three peaks at 398.4, 399.7, and 401.1 eV belonging to the B–N, N–C, and N-H bonds, respectively. Fig. 1h depicts the B 1 s core level spectrum that is deconvoluted into three peaks at 190.6, 192.3, and 193.4 eV ascribed to the B–N, B–O in N-B-O, and B-O bonding in B2O3, respectively. The XPS results corroborated the TEM, UV–vis, and FTIR data confirmed the successful preparation of BNQDs [47].

In order to investigate the performance of BNQDs used as LDI-MS matrix, the "matrix-first" and "mixing-first" strategies were employed to process the samples for MALDI-MS analysis. In the mass spectrum obtained by the mixing-first method (Fig. S1a), the signal at 227.14 m/z, associated with BPA, was 150% higher than that obtained by the matrix-first method (Fig. S1b), and 210% larger than that recorded by the sample-first method (Fig. S1c). Consequently, the mixing-first approach was used for MALDI-MS analysis.

The BNQDs exhibited a clear background mass spectrum, as shown in Fig. S2a. Fig. S2b displays the performance of the BNQDs as a MALDI inorganic matrix for BPA detection. As controls, we assessed the MALDI-MS performance of positive ion mode organic matrices of CHCA and DHB for BPA analysis. Background interference peaks were prominent for both CHCA and DHB (Figs. S2c-3d), thereby reducing the signal strengths of the analytes. By contrast, the signal intensities measured using the BNQDs matrix were 10 and 20 times greater than those recorded with respectively CHCA and DHB, respectively. The results of negative ion mode matrices, such as 9AA (Fig. S2e), NEDC (Fig. S2f), Norharmane (Fig. S2g), and DAN (Fig. S2h), were also investigated. The signal intensities measured using the BNQDs matrix are generally 50, 25, 20 and 20 times greater than those recorded with respectively the above four negative ion mode matrices. These findings demonstrate the high chemical stability, strong laser absorption, and energy transfer potential of the BNQDs as a MALDI-MS matrix.

The BNQDs matrix allowed to identify compounds with varied amounts and positions of hydroxyl groups, demonstrating its outstanding detection capacity. As expected, 2,2-DP ([M-H] −=193.14), 2,2-PP ([M-H] −=211.14), and BPA ([M-H] −=227.14) were detected (Fig. 2a). The [M-H]− characteristic ions of 1-hydroxyl-AQ, 1,5-dihydroxyl-AQ, and AQ, were observed at m/z = 223.20, 239.21, and 207.20, respectively (Fig. 2b). Additionally, the higher hydroxyl group content of analytes yielded stronger MS signal for the analytes in the following order: 2,2-DP< 2,2-PP<BPA, and AQ< 1-hydroxyl-AQ< 1,5-dihydroxyl-AQ. Signal enhancement with an increase in the number of hydroxyl groups in the mass spectra of these compounds was not observed using commercial DHB, as illustrated in Figs. 2c and 2d. These findings confirmed that an increase in the number of hydroxyl groups in analytes contributed to a more robust interaction between the BNQDs matrix and the analytes. Intermolecular hydrogen bonds were strengthened by the hydroxyl groups of the analyte interacting with the hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the BNQDs, yielding strong MALDI-MS signals [48]. This method may not have particularly high selectivity for other compounds with both hydroxyl and amino groups. To find an inorganic matrix only react with the bisphenol A but not with the non-target compounds having hydroxyl and amino groups is quite challenging. Other sample preparation methods and matrix, for example BPA molecular imprinting polymers, might have such selectivity, but the problem of hydrogen bond between molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) and other compounds with hydroxyl and amino groups need to be solved before applied prior to the MALDI-MS analysis. Moreover, the template bleeding should also be avoided especially for image application.

The analysis of small molecules in real samples frequently presents the challenge of dealing with high salt concentrations. The reduced ionization efficiency and lowered analyte signals may have resulted from the presence of high salt concentrations. KCl was used for BPA analysis to simulate the conditions present in the real samples and examine the salt tolerance of the BNQDs as a MALDI-MS matrix. Fig. S3 shows that when the concentration of KCl was increased to 500 mM, the strength of the BPA signal decreased to 88.6% of its initial value, demonstrating that BNQDs as a MALDI-MS matrix have the advantage of strong salt tolerance.

The BNQDs matrix was evaluated primarily based on its repeatability. The shot-to-shot (n = 30) relative standard deviation (RSD) for BPA, which was 4.64%, is computed using the signals shown in Fig. S4a. The sample-to-sample (n = 30) RSD for BPA is 6.62%, as shown in Fig. S4b. The coefficient of variance (CV) was used to assess the reproducibility. The MS intensity of BPA was recorded using five shots from different locations on one sample spot, and six samples were investigated. As shown in Table S1, the CV values ranged from 2.67% to 8.67%. These findings indicate that employing BNQDs as the matrix leads to reliable signal reproducibility.

Fig. 3a depicts the quantitative analysis of BPA using the BNQDs matrix. The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantitation (LOQs) were determined according to the quality of BPA with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 and 10, respectively. The LOD was 0.05 nM, and LOQ was found to be 0.20 nM over a wide linear range (0.25–250 nM). Standard addition technique was applied for the determination of BPA. Table 1 displays the analytical results of BPA in real bottled water, wastewater and seawater samples. Recovery was assessed in the samples at BPA concentrations of 1, 10, and 50 nM. The analyte recovery range was from 87.80% to 108.5%, with RSD values of less than 10%. Fig. 3b shows that BNQDs-assisted MALDI-MS was able to detect 9.50 nM BPA in wastewater. HPLC-MS/MS analysis revealed that BPA retention time was 3.11 min, yielding a concentration of 9.35 nM (Fig. 3c). The comparable results from the two methods indicate that BNQDs are a remarkable substrate for the MALDI-MS determination of BPA in real samples. A comparison of previously published quantum sized materials for detecting BPA is presented in Table S2 [49–53]. It can be found that the proposed approach has a relatively low detection limit. In addition, the BNQDs was prepared through an easy hydrothermal method. Compared with these methods, BNQDs assisted LDI-TOF MS showed the advantages of low-cost, simple sample pre-treatment, rapid and high-throughput analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spatial distribution of BPA analyzed by BNQDs-assisted MALDI-MSI
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the possible application of the developed BNQDs matrix in MALDI-MSI, epoxy films, and baby pacifiers were used for BPA imaging.

Because BNQDs are extremely sensitive and specific to phenolic molecules, in situ BPA imaging of the epoxy films was performed. A thickness BNQDs thin film about 0.2 µm was obtained using the spraying method (Fig. S5) and a spatial resolution of 100 µm was selected for the experiment (Fig. S6). The SEM image of the deposited BNQDs matrix indicated that a homogeneous layer of BNQDs was deposited using a manual sprayer (Fig. S7). We have also carried a test using BPA sprayed blank sample using HTX sprayer. The BPA should be uniformed distributed in the surface of sample. After BNQDs was deposited on the sample, 10 shots were taken from different locations on the sample area. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values for spot repeatability were less than 10%, demonstrating the homogeneity and reliability of the manual sprayer application for the deposition of the BNQDs. After coating an ITO glass with the epoxy resin to form a thin film, thermal treatment of the epoxy films was carried out for 0–72 h at 130 °C ( Fig. 4a, d). The precision RSD (n = 3) of the absolute intensity was < 6.5% for imaging. MALDI-MSI using the BNQDs matrix was applied as an in situ and non-destructive process, rather than a complicated dissolution and separation process, to assess the molecules generated upon laser excitation of the epoxy films (Fig. 4b). There are discernible BPA signal distributions at m/z = 227.14 across the epoxy film, with the spatial distribution increasing with treatment duration. The signal intensities for the epoxy without aging treatment (0 h) were typically less than 5%, except for a few sporadically distributed high-intensity spots. The signal intensities of the peak at m/z = 227.14 were increased by ∼50 times upon increasing the aging time to 72 h, indicating an increase in BPA generation. This proposed MALDI-MSI methodology provides direct visualization of BPA generation. By contrast, DHB showed no discernible increasing or decreasing trend (Fig. 4c). BNQDs-assisted MALDI-MSI was used to detect BPA in baby pacifiers. There was little BPA distribution on the samples sliced at room temperature, as indicated by the red line for the baby used pacifier in Fig. 4e. No obvious BPA detection in the blank pacifier after boiling in water for an hour (Fig. S8). For pacifiers used by babies after one year, comparable results for BPA distribution were recorded in the pacifier maintained at room temperature (Fig. 4f). After boiling the pacifier for an hour, the MS images at m/z = 227.14 were prominent (Fig. 4g). Moreover, time-dependent study of BPA release was conducted. BPA is released fast in the first half hour, and the BPA distribution tended to balance out after1 hour of boiling (Fig.S9). Just as we suspected, owing to the hydrogen bonding interactions between BPA and BNQDs, the more harmful BPA presented the significantly higher signal. These findings demonstrated that BNQDs-assisted MALDI-MSI produced the high-resolution imaging of BPA in real samples.

Fig. 5 depicts a possible synergistic desorption/ionization mechanism for the BNQDs. Three factors contributed to BNQDs' high performance in MALDI-MS analysis. (i) High electron transfer capacity. BNQDs exhibit good and broad optical absorption in the 300–700 nm range, suggesting their suitability as an inorganic matrix. This feature helps in absorbing the laser energy and then transfer the energy to target analytes for desorption/ionization [30]. (ii) Chemo-selectivity. The ability to selectively target hydroxyl-containing BPA molecules is ascribed to the abundance of hydroxyl and amino groups on the BNQDs’ surface. Fig. 5 depicts the two types of hydrogen bonds (i.e., –OH⋅⋅⋅O– and –NH⋅⋅⋅O–) between BNQDs and BPA, which are important in the adsorption mechanism. The binding energies of –OH and –NH on BNQDs were explored and interpreted using density functional theory (DFT), and were − 31.19 and − 37.29 kJ⋅mol−1, respectively. This phenomenon emphasises the importance of considering hydrogen-bonding interactions as stronger non-covalent interaction forces when developing inorganic matrices for MALDI-MS [48]. (iii) Homogeneous MALDI hotspot distribution. The small size and improved water dispersibility of BNQDs, owing to the presence of functional groups (hydroxyl and amino groups) on their surface. They are valuable because they can produce uniform aqueous solutions that cocrystallize with analytes, thereby increasing the probability of detection. A homogeneous matrix solution also allows for greater reproducibility of results and low spot-to-spot variation compared to insoluble nanoparticle matrices [54]. The underlying mechanism provides a resonable explanation for matrix/analyte ionization and interaction, indicating the BNQDs practicality as an inorganic matrix for MALDI-MS analysis of environmental pollutants with high sensitivity.

In conclusion, the BNQDs matrix was used for MALDI-MS and MSI to detect BPA in water, epoxy films, and pacifier samples. The BNQDs were more sensitive and displayed lower background interference peaks than commonly used organic matrices, such as HCCA, DHB, 9AA, NEDC, Norharmane, and DAN. The BNQDs matrix exhibited appropriate salt resistance and high reproducibility. The technique allowed the detection of BPA with a low detection limit. The epoxy films and pacifier samples were analyzed using the BNQDs matrix, and high-quality images of the BPA distribution were obtained. A possible mechanism for the desorption/ionization process assisted by the BNQDs is proposed. Hence, BNQDs can be developed as a nanoscale matrix for MALDI-MS and for rapid BPA detection. This is expected to inspire future research on the analysis of environmental pollutants using BNQDs-assisted MALDI quantitative imaging.

The bisphenol A (BPA) residues in environmental water and plastic products may have harmful effects on the human body. This study presents the boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) as a novel matrix in matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry and imaging (MALDI-MS & MSI) for BPA. Hydroxyl and amino groups are abundant in BNQDs, allowing them to form hydrogen bonds with BPA and increase their ionization and chemoselectivity. The method can detect BPA at nM level in environmental water, and used for the non-destructive and in situ mapping of BPA in plastics and pacifiers used by infants.

Yanfang Zhao: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Rabah Boukherroub: Writing – review & editing. Lu Liu & Huizhi Li: Validation. Ru-Song Zhao: Project administration. Qin Wei: Software. Xiang Yu: Supervision, Resources. Xiangfeng Chen: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The Pilot Project on the Integration of Science Education and Production (2022PY024 and 2022PYI013). The authors would like to thank profeesor Zhang from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the language editing.
